results.title: Call Her Mommy
results.author: Angelina Chapin
results.content:
At first, McKayla didn’t care that her boyfriend worked for Instacart. He had just finished college, after all, and she was in her senior year, busy writing papers and trying to figure out her adult life. But once McKayla landed a full-time job as a reporter at a local newspaper, just a few months after graduating, she started to see things differently. She moved into her own studio apartment and still found the time to freelance for extra cash. Meanwhile, he was living at home with his parents and in no rush to put his economics and history degree to use. “I would send him links to jobs every couple of weeks and he wouldn’t open them,” she said. “He just wasn’t motivated to find anything.” She picked up the bill when they went out to dinner and bought them tickets to see Chris Rock and Bill Maher (the latter, she says, was her boyfriend’s choice). Sometimes she’d help him deliver other people’s groceries during their date nights.
McKayla’s parents, who had watched their daughter become a first-generation college grad, were not impressed. A couple summers ago, they offered to buy her Eras Tour tickets if she broke it off with him. Instead, she helped her boyfriend land a job with Kamala Harris’s campaign by getting a well-connected friend to flag his application; after that, he started working for a state representative in Philadelphia. Now he makes $42,000, about half of what she does, but their five-year-old relationship still feels stuck. They’ve talked about moving in together but McKayla wants to be in a building with a rooftop pool and gym, amenities he can’t afford. “I’m not going to live in a walk-up with no laundry,” she says. But it’s more than just the money. McKayla worries that he lacks ambition. “I do wish he himself was motivated to find a higher paying job in politics,” she says. “He doesn’t know what it takes to build a home. I’m always the driving force behind him.” It makes her feel more like a nagging mother than a girlfriend.
Since the dawn of paychecks, women have made less than men. But while the overall wage gap persists, some Gen-Z women are living through a more hopeful reality. They are ten points more likely than men their age to get a college degree and are more likely than their male peers to find work once they graduate. While pink-coded jobs in therapy and health care are growing at record-high rates, there have been hiring dips in traditionally male-dominated fields like tech, finance, and blue-collar trades like construction and plumbing. A few years ago, a Pew study found that in a handful of cities, like New York, Los Angeles, and Washington, D.C., young women are actually earning more than their male peers. With that extra money they are also buying almost twice as many homes as young men and saving for retirement at higher rates. Women of this generation are more likely than any other time in history to date a man who makes less than them — or who makes nothing at all.
Young men, I assumed, must be humiliated by this state of affairs, their masculinity in tatters. But for the most part, they seem comfortable with being pampered and paid for — maybe a little too comfortable. On TikTok, they call their girlfriends “mommies” and refer to themselves as “sugar babies.” “My girl makes more than me, and it’s awesome,” one guy told me. “She’ll be buying me all the fancy tools I want,” said another. I recently came across a Reddit post by a 23-year-old who wasn’t sure how he felt about making less than his girlfriend. All the commenters told him to get over it. “Bro just love your girl, and do what you can,” one said. “If she’s taking you with her on the trips, you’re gucci.”
Women are less thrilled about being treated like ATMs. Men, they say, are in their “princess era.” “I feel like when I go out with a guy that I need to show up with flowers and I need to open his car door,” said one woman. “Like, ‘Anything for you, princess!’” Things are so dire that on TikTok, women are lip-syncing to a new country song about how “If those fairy tales were re-written today / The tramp would wine and dine the lady / And then he’d make her pay.” “All the girls in my life are investing in real estate, they’re making ‘fuck you’ money in sales, they run a business,” one woman said in a car confessional. “And all the guys that we meet our age are just not.” The women I spoke to don’t mind being breadwinners. But they feel like babysitters too.
Naomi’s worst fear was turning into her boyfriend’s mother. But in truth, the 24-year-old paid for everything, helped him with his résumé, and made sure he was looking for jobs. He reassured her that he’d make some changes, like going to therapy and showing up on time when they hung out. Instead, things got worse. While she cobbled together a six-figure income working two retail jobs, he talked about applying to law school while lying in bed at her apartment on the Upper East Side, watching Reels on his phone.
It wasn’t a big deal that he couldn’t buy Naomi dinner at Le Veau d’Or. But she didn’t like how he used money as an excuse to shirk basic responsibilities. He never planned dates and didn’t even get her a birthday present. “If you can’t afford something, write me a love letter,” she says. “He could have literally made me Kraft mac and cheese.” They talked about moving in together, but Naomi couldn’t justify the cost. “If I wanted to finance a human being,” she says, “I’d have a kid.”
But perhaps the most enraging part of their now-finished relationship is that Naomi’s boyfriend used politics to justify his apathy. He considered himself a communist, read Marx in his spare time, and was part of a friend group full of other jobless men who found it embarrassing to care about work. “They hate the finance bro,” Naomi says, “But in their push to reject that, they’re now doing nothing.” McKayla, who requested a pseudonym along with the other women in this story, has clocked a similar phenomenon. She says her boyfriend and his friends are “super-granola crunchy liberals” who see striving for power as anti-feminist. They want to separate themselves from predatory CEOs and men on the right who think women are working pretend “email jobs” and shouldn’t even have the right to vote. “They think treating women respectfully cannot exist with being a hyperambitious provider,” she says. “They think if you make six figures, you’ll suddenly wake up and hate women.”
One 27-year-old, who describes himself as a “modern leftist,” says that he and his friends are more concerned with finding jobs that align with their values than with getting rich. “A lot of the women I know are hyperambitious,” he says. “A lot of the men they date are not.” I heard from one guy who said that while he has no problem with his partner making more money than him, he always tries to pay in public, lest a barista judge him for “taking advantage of his girlfriend.” Another man, whose girlfriend earns twice as much as him, told me he provides in other ways: His work-life balance means he has the “emotional availability” to comfort his girlfriend when she comes home from a stressful day at work. “I’m a caretaker to some degree,” he says. “What I bring to the relationship is arguably not traditionally masculine, but it is fulfilling and valuable.”
The women I spoke with had a different definition of “valuable.” While their boyfriends struggled to chip in for groceries, they also seemed strangely reticent to contribute in other ways — like, you know, doing the dishes or handling the laundry. Jessica couldn’t seem to escape this kind of guy. A few years ago, while the then-19-year-old juggled an engineering internship with a part-time job, she dated someone who was supposedly in the process of applying to med school. Not only did Jessica pay for everything, she organized all their dates as well. “I would buy the candles, do all the cooking, pick the music, and pick a time,” she says. “All he had to do was show up.” More than once she heard him boast to his friends that he didn’t have to worry about money because “Jessica’s such a mommy.” At the club, he’d ask her to flirt with single guys to score free drinks. She broke up with him right before graduating from college and soon started dating a fellow engineering classmate who talked a big game about getting a corporate job. “At that point I was like, ‘I just want someone who will pay for things sometimes,’” Jessica says. “He was like, ‘Don’t worry, baby girl, I’ll take care of you.’” Then he moved back into his parents’ place while complaining about how tough the job market was.
She worked long hours at her consulting job and still picked up the bill when he felt like having a $300 meal (“he had expensive taste,” she says). “I was again trapped in this role of planning activities and doing chores because he would say he ‘didn’t have the capacity,’” Jessica says. “At a certain point it felt like there’s nothing this guy is adding to my life that I can’t just provide for myself.” She dumped him, too, and found a new man who, despite making less money than her, at least brings something to the table. Now that Jessica is getting her master’s degree while running a group focused on educating people about climate change, she needs help getting the rest of her life together. Her boyfriend recently quit his job to go back to school and spends his free time driving her to work events, booking her yoga classes to help her relax, and packing her leftover pasta for lunch. “He’s like, ‘You’re going to change the world,’’’ she says. “‘So I need to help you do that.’”
McKayla resents her boyfriend’s nonchalance. As someone who didn’t have money growing up, she’s clawed her way toward a quality of life she refuses to give up. Her boyfriend, who was raised in an upper-middle-class suburb, is not as forward thinking. When they spoke about moving in together, he told McKayla he has only $1,000 in savings. “I was like, ‘Oh, he isn’t taking this seriously,’” she says. “He just thought that he would somehow come up with the money we need for first and last months’ rent and a security deposit.” When she talked about sending their future children to an Ivy League school, he responded, “Why would we do that?” “I just wish he would exhibit more hunger,” she says. “I know what I could do with the leg up he has, and he just sits on it.”
For many young women, ambition doesn’t feel like a choice. If they want careers that pay the bills, they can’t afford to lay around, putting off job applications and 401(k) contributions while bragging about their perfect politics. They need to buckle down, fast. While it might seem like their boyfriends are lagging far behind, they’ll probably catch up anyway. Historically, the wage gap tends to be narrowest before the age of 35; it gets much wider later on, especially after women have kids. While fathers tend to get a pay bump, women make half as much as they did before becoming mothers. It’s one thing to pick up the tab for your boyfriend who’s genuinely struggling to find work. It’s another to subsidize rent for a guy who spends leisurely afternoons on the couch because he has a feeling that, eventually, he’ll get what he wants.
At least McKayla’s not going to wait for her boyfriend to get himself together. “If the New York Times called me tomorrow and was like, ‘Can you move here in 48 hours,’ I don’t even think I would tell him,” she says. “I would just do it.”
More Stories About Ambition
- How 40 Striving Young Women See Ambition
- Lindsay Hubbard Is Ambitious and Activated
- ‘My Terrible Boss Is Holding Me Back. Can I Just Quit Without Notice?’
results.summary:
As Gen-Z women increasingly outpace their male peers in education and earnings, many are navigating complex relationship dynamics where they serve as both primary breadwinners and domestic managers.

### Key Points
- Gen-Z women are currently 10% more likely than their male counterparts to hold college degrees and are securing employment at higher rates.
- Data from Pew Research indicates that young women in major cities like New York and Los Angeles are frequently earning more than their male peers.
- Many young men are adopting a "princess era" mindset, often relying on their partners for financial support while citing political or emotional reasons for their lack of career ambition.
- Women report feeling burdened by the "mommy" role, as they often manage household chores, career planning, and financial responsibilities for underemployed partners.
- Despite the current trend of female-led finances, the national wage gap historically widens after age 35, particularly following the birth of children.

### Why it Matters
This shift in earning power is fundamentally altering traditional relationship expectations and creating friction between ambitious women and partners who prioritize lifestyle over financial stability. As women continue to out-earn men early in their careers, the social and economic pressure to act as both provider and caregiver is forcing many to reevaluate the long-term viability of their partnerships.

results.title: House Democrats Investigate Whether Epstein Was Foreign Agent
results.author: Hafiz Rashid
results.content:
Convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein may have been acting as a foreign agent for multiple countries, and House Democrats are launching an investigation.
Representative Jamie Raskin sent a letter to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Director of National Intelligence nominee Jay Clayton, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Monday asking for records from the Trump administration on Epstein’s international ties.
“Our examination of available evidence has already revealed damning indications of Mr. Epstein’s activities on behalf of foreign states,” Raskin wrote in the letter, pointing out public evidence of the billionaire’s ties to Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Russia, and Israel.
In addition, Epstein “repeatedly capitalized on his long-term friendship with President Donald Trump to sign up business from these and other foreign governments and actors in the aftermath of President Trump’s first election, without ever registering as a foreign agent under the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA),” Raskin added. “By advocating and facilitating on behalf of foreign countries without filing the mandatory disclosures, Mr. Epstein almost certainly violated FARA. His actions also create serious questions about the extent to which he wielded his ties to President Trump in dealings with foreign governments.”
The Department of Justice still has not released 2.5 million files related to Epstein, and Raskin, the ranking member of the House Judiciary Committee, believes that some of them concern his foreign dealings. Raskin also asked in the letter for the DOJ to respond in writing as to whether Epstein was ever investigated for being a foreign agent.
“Jeffrey Epstein never registered as a foreign agent. Yet numerous recently released documents now show he acted aggressively on behalf of multiple foreign governments, including several with interests adverse to the United States, in order to influence policies of the first Trump Administration,” the letter states.
Trump’s Justice Department has been under fire from Republicans and Democrats alike over its handling of the Epstein files, from its selective redactions to its barely meeting with Epstein’s victims. Investigating Epstein’s foreign ties could reveal more damning decisions from administration officials.
results.summary:
House Democrats led by Representative Jamie Raskin are launching an investigation into whether convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein acted as an unregistered foreign agent for multiple international governments.

### Key Points
- Representative Jamie Raskin requested records from the Department of Justice, the State Department, and the Director of National Intelligence regarding Epstein’s foreign ties.
- The investigation focuses on allegations that Epstein lobbied for Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Russia, and Israel without registering under the Foreign Agents Registration Act.
- Raskin claims Epstein leveraged his personal relationship with Donald Trump to secure business deals with foreign actors following the 2016 presidential election.
- The Department of Justice currently holds approximately 2.5 million unreleased files related to Epstein that may contain evidence of these international activities.
- The inquiry seeks to determine if the Trump administration failed to properly investigate Epstein’s potential status as a foreign agent.

### Why it Matters
This investigation could expose significant national security vulnerabilities and potential corruption involving the influence of foreign governments on the first Trump administration. It also places renewed pressure on the Department of Justice to release millions of classified documents that have remained hidden from public scrutiny.

results.title: Before You Build Custom Software, Answer These 5 Questions
results.author: Bidhan Baruah
results.content:
Opinions expressed by Entrepreneur contributors are their own.
Key Takeaways
- Custom software development is a massive commitment that requires more than capital. u003cbru003e
- The build vs buy software debate always comes down to your core competitive advantage. u003cbru003e
- A solid enterprise software strategy accounts for long-term maintenance instead of the initial launch alone. u003cbru003e
- Protect your software investment by aligning development goals with strict business outcomes.
Many enterprise leaders fall in love with the idea of a bespoke digital solution. They want a platform tailored exactly to their unique workflows. But diving into custom software development without a rigid plan is a fast track to burning capital.
The reality is far less romantic than the pitch. Building a platform from scratch is hard, unpredictable and expensive. With Grand View Research projecting the global enterprise software market to surpass $300 billion in 2026, you need a bulletproof enterprise software strategy before writing a single line of code.
Too many companies realize they made a massive mistake halfway through the build. You can avoid these expensive missteps by asking your leadership team five brutal questions.
Does this create a true competitive advantage?
The first filter for any tech investment is competitive differentiation. If the software handles standard backend operations, you should not be building it. Things like payroll, basic CRM functions and inventory management are already solved problems.
Building from scratch only makes sense when the tool becomes your actual competitive moat. Your competitors should look at this platform and realize they cannot buy the same capability off a shelf. Walk away from the project if it fails to make your team faster or smarter.
Can we tolerate the true cost of ownership?
Founders often treat software like buying a house. They think about the upfront cost and ignore the ongoing property taxes. In the tech world, maintenance is your property tax.
The initial launch is the down payment on your software investment. You will face server costs, security patches, third-party API updates and constant bug fixes. Plan to spend roughly a fifth of your original build cost every year to keep the lights on.
Funding the initial launch without holding cash back for upkeep guarantees failure. Scaling a business means your internal tech must stretch with you. That means funding new API connections year after year.
It also means spending to onboard every new hire onto a closed system they have never seen before.
Will existing solutions get us close enough?
This is where the build vs buy software debate gets uncomfortable. Enterprise leaders often reject existing platforms because they lack one or two specific features. They decide to spend millions to get that final 10% of functionality.
Modern commercial tools come equipped with robust APIs. You can often stitch together three different subscription platforms to create a seamless workflow for a fraction of the cost of building a unified system from scratch.
Before greenlighting a custom build, you must exhaust every available commercial option. Look hard at existing platforms. See if adjusting your internal processes to fit a pre-built tool is cheaper than building a tool to fit your processes.
Compromising on a few minor workflows is usually smarter than taking on the risk of a ground-up build.
Do we have the internal team to steer this?
You cannot hand a brief to an engineering team and walk away. Successful custom projects require intense daily collaboration between your business leaders and the development team.
Your company needs a dedicated product owner. This person must have the authority to make rapid decisions and the time to test new iterations every week. If your leadership team is already stretched too thin, the software will suffer.
Without strong internal guidance, developers will guess what you want. Those guesses are rarely correct and always expensive to fix.
Your product owner also has to drive internal adoption once the tool goes live. A million-dollar platform becomes worthless the minute your team decides to fall back on their comfortable spreadsheets.
Defining the exact metrics for success
Vague goals cannot justify this level of financial risk. You need an enterprise software strategy built on strict, quantifiable targets before signing any checks.
Determine what success looks like on day one. Pinpoint whether you want to slash processing time, drop customer churn by a specific percentage or destroy a known operational bottleneck. Get those numbers on paper.
Building a new tool without directly connecting it to hard business results means you are paying for an expensive digital toy.
Committing to custom software development changes the entire trajectory of your business operations. It is not another vendor purchase. It tests your patience and drains your capital.
The build vs buy software debate deserves intense scrutiny. Making your leadership team confront these five realities strips away the romanticized hype of building from scratch.
It safeguards your cash. More importantly, it guarantees that any custom platform you fund will improve your business.
Key Takeaways
- Custom software development is a massive commitment that requires more than capital. u003cbru003e
- The build vs buy software debate always comes down to your core competitive advantage. u003cbru003e
- A solid enterprise software strategy accounts for long-term maintenance instead of the initial launch alone. u003cbru003e
- Protect your software investment by aligning development goals with strict business outcomes.
Many enterprise leaders fall in love with the idea of a bespoke digital solution. They want a platform tailored exactly to their unique workflows. But diving into custom software development without a rigid plan is a fast track to burning capital.
The reality is far less romantic than the pitch. Building a platform from scratch is hard, unpredictable and expensive. With Grand View Research projecting the global enterprise software market to surpass $300 billion in 2026, you need a bulletproof enterprise software strategy before writing a single line of code.
Too many companies realize they made a massive mistake halfway through the build. You can avoid these expensive missteps by asking your leadership team five brutal questions.
results.summary:
Enterprise leaders should carefully evaluate the risks and long-term costs of custom software development to ensure investments provide a genuine competitive advantage rather than unnecessary financial strain.

### Key Points
- Grand View Research projects the global enterprise software market will exceed $300 billion by 2026.
- Custom development is only recommended if the software creates a unique competitive moat that cannot be replicated by off-the-shelf solutions.
- Companies must budget approximately 20% of the initial build cost annually to cover ongoing maintenance, security patches, and API updates.
- Organizations should exhaust all commercial software options and consider integrating existing platforms before committing to a ground-up build.
- Successful projects require a dedicated internal product owner to manage daily collaboration and ensure the software aligns with quantifiable business outcomes.

### Why it Matters
Choosing between building custom software and purchasing existing solutions is a high-stakes decision that directly impacts a company's capital reserves and operational efficiency. A disciplined strategy prevents businesses from wasting resources on redundant tools while ensuring that technology investments drive measurable growth and long-term scalability.

results.title: U.S. stocks set for higher open as strong earnings boost market sentiment: Dow Jones, S&P, Nasdaq, Wall Street Futures
results.author: Fiona Craig
results.content:
© Shutterstock
U.S. stock futures pointed to a firmer open on Tuesday as investors looked to recover from the previous session’s decline, with upbeat corporate earnings encouraging buying across several sectors.
Market sentiment improved after a series of better-than-expected quarterly reports, prompting traders to return to stocks following recent weakness.
Among the early gainers, 3M (NYSE:MMM) advanced more than 7% in pre-market trading after reporting second-quarter earnings and revenue ahead of expectations while raising its full-year outlook.
General Motors (NYSE:GM) also attracted buying interest after delivering quarterly results that surpassed analyst forecasts and increasing its 2026 financial guidance.
Meanwhile, Novartis (NYSE:NVS) traded higher after the Swiss pharmaceutical company reported second-quarter results that exceeded market expectations.
Technology stocks also provided support, with Nasdaq 100 futures rising approximately 1.2% ahead of the opening bell.
Trading activity could remain restrained despite the positive start, as investors await a busy week of earnings from several of the market’s largest technology companies.
Results from Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL), IBM (NYSE:IBM) and Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) are expected later this week and could provide fresh insight into artificial intelligence spending and broader corporate demand.
Gains in equity markets were tempered by another sharp increase in crude oil prices.
U.S. crude futures climbed about 2% as military exchanges between the United States and Iran continued, adding to concerns that higher energy costs could keep inflation elevated and complicate the interest-rate outlook.
The major U.S. indexes initially rallied on Monday before reversing course as investors reassessed geopolitical risks and interest-rate expectations.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 307.16 points, or 0.6%, to 51,839.26. The S&P 500 declined 14.41 points, or 0.2%, to 7,443.28, while the Nasdaq Composite slipped 12.17 points, or 0.1%, to 25,508.07.
Housing stocks led Monday’s declines as rising Treasury yields pressured interest-rate-sensitive sectors, while pharmaceutical, biotechnology, transportation and healthcare shares also weakened. Software stocks, however, outperformed the broader market.
Separately, the Conference Board reported that its Leading Economic Index declined 0.2% in June after increasing 0.1% in May, slightly weaker than economists had expected.

U.S. stock futures are trending higher on Tuesday as strong quarterly earnings reports from major corporations help investors recover from recent market declines and persistent geopolitical uncertainty.

### Key Points
- 3M shares rose over 7% in pre-market trading following better-than-expected second-quarter revenue and an increased full-year outlook.
- General Motors stock gained after the automaker surpassed analyst forecasts and raised its 2026 financial guidance.
- Novartis reported second-quarter results that exceeded market expectations, contributing to positive sentiment in the pharmaceutical sector.
- Nasdaq 100 futures climbed 1.2% as investors prepare for upcoming earnings reports from Alphabet, IBM, and Tesla.
- U.S. crude oil futures increased by 2% amid ongoing military tensions between the United States and Iran.
- The Conference Board’s Leading Economic Index fell 0.2% in June, reflecting a slightly weaker economic outlook than economists previously anticipated.

### Why it Matters
Strong corporate earnings are currently providing a necessary buffer against rising energy costs and geopolitical risks that threaten to keep inflation elevated. Investors are now closely monitoring upcoming reports from major technology firms to gauge the sustainability of artificial intelligence spending and broader market demand.

results.title: Estée Lauder discloses data breach tied to Oracle EBS vulnerability
results.author: Sinisa Markovic
results.content:
Estée Lauder discloses data breach tied to Oracle EBS vulnerability
Cosmetics company Estée Lauder disclosed a data breach tied to a vulnerability in Oracle E-Business Suite (EBS) used for the company’s human resources operations.
Estée Lauder is one of the largest beauty companies in the world, known for its prestige skincare, makeup, fragrance, and haircare brands.
“We became aware of a cybersecurity issue involving a vulnerability in the Oracle E-Business Suite system which is used by the Estee Lauder Companies for HR management purposes,” the company said in the notice.
“On June 19, 2026, we determined through our investigation that, on or around August 9, 2025, an unauthorized third party gained access to the Oracle E-Business Suite system and obtained personal information of certain individuals.”
The data taken varied from person to person. According to the notification, it included names, postal and email addresses, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, passport numbers, bank account numbers, health information, and employment records such as performance evaluations and payroll history.
Following the discovery, Estée Lauder said it brought in outside cybersecurity experts, notified law enforcement, and added safeguards to the system. The company is also offering 24 months of free identity monitoring through Kroll, with a deadline of October 31, 2026.
“Please remain vigilant in protecting against identity theft and fraud, including monitoring your accounts, account statements, and credit reports for signs of suspicious activity,” the company added.
Although the notification does not identify the threat actor responsible, the date of the breach aligns with the start of the mass-exploitation campaign targeting Oracle E-Business Suite via CVE-2025-61882.
In October 2025, Google and Mandiant researchers confirmed that the Cl0p extortion gang had exploited multiple Oracle E-Business Suite vulnerabilities, including the CVE-2025-61882 zero-day, to steal large amounts of data from several victims in August 2025.
The flaw allowed unauthenticated attackers with network access to remotely execute code over HTTP, affecting Oracle E-Business Suite versions 12.2.3 through 12.2.14.
Oracle released fixes for CVE-2025-61882 on October 4, 2025.
results.summary:
Estée Lauder has disclosed a significant data breach involving the theft of sensitive employee information after attackers exploited a critical vulnerability in its Oracle E-Business Suite HR system.

### Key Points
- Unauthorized parties accessed the Oracle E-Business Suite system on or around August 9, 2025, through a vulnerability identified as CVE-2025-61882.
- Stolen data includes Social Security numbers, passport details, bank account information, health records, and employment history.
- The breach is linked to a mass-exploitation campaign by the Cl0p extortion gang, which targeted Oracle systems globally throughout August 2025.
- Estée Lauder is providing 24 months of free identity monitoring services through Kroll to affected individuals until the October 31, 2026, deadline.
- Oracle released security patches for the affected software versions, ranging from 12.2.3 to 12.2.14, on October 4, 2025.

### Why it Matters
This incident highlights the severe risks posed by unpatched vulnerabilities in enterprise resource planning software used to manage sensitive human resources data. The exposure of highly personal information underscores the critical need for organizations to prioritize rapid patch management to prevent large-scale data exfiltration by sophisticated cybercriminal groups.
